title: Volume and surface area word problems
description: Solve word problems involving volume and surface area involving 3D shapes, including composite figures.
topic_path: Volume and surface area word problems

assessment_items.question_content:
A candy store called "Sugar" built a giant hollow sugar cube out of wood to hang above the entrance to their store. It took $13.5\text{ m}^2$ of material to build the cube.

**What is the volume inside the giant sugar cube?**  
*Give an exact answer (do not round).*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}^3$
hints:
The amount of material it took to build the cube is the same as the surface area. Since a  cube has $6$ congruent faces we can divide to find the area of one face.

$\dfrac{13.5}{6} = \purpleD{2.25}\text{ m}^2$

$\purpleD{2.25}\text{ m}^2$ is the area for each face of the cube.

[[☃ image 1]]

Each face is square and has an area of $\purpleD{2.25}\text{ m}^2$. To find the length of each side of the square we can take the square root.

$\sqrt{\purpleD{2.25}} = 1.5$

The length of each side of the square is $1.5$ meters.
To find the volume of the cube, we can use the formula $V=lwh$.

$\begin{align}V& = lwh \\\\
V & = 1.5\cdot1.5\cdot1.5 \\\\
V & = 3.375\end{align}$
The volume of the giant sugar cube is $3.375$ meters$^3$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Hassan built a fence around a square yard. It took $48\text{ m}^2$ of lumber to build the fence. The fence is $1.5$ meters tall. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the area of the yard inside the fence?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}^2$
hints:
The fence outlines a square yard, so all $4$ sections of the fence are congruent rectangles. To find the area of each section of the fence, we can divide.

$\dfrac{48}{4} = \purpleD{12}\text{ m}^2$

$\purpleD{12}\text{ m}^2$ is the area of $1$ section of the fence.

[[☃ image 1]]

We know the fence is $\maroonD{1.5}$ meters tall and the area of each rectangular fence section is $\purpleD{12}\text{ m}^2$. We can use these values to find the length of each fence section.

$\begin{align}\purpleD A & = lw \\\\
\purpleD{12} & = l\cdot \maroonD{1.5} \\\\
8 & = l \end{align}$

Each section is $8$ meters long. The yard is in the shape of a square, so we can multiply $2$ sides together to find the area inside the yard.

$8 \cdot 8= 64$
The area of the yard is $64\text{ m}^2$.

options.alt: A square yard surrounded by four fences along its four sides. The height of the fence is 1.5 meters. The area of the fence is 12 square meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Oliver is building a box kite. The frame of the kite has the dimensions shown. He covered the sides of the kite (but not the top and bottom) with $7200$ cm$^2$ of lightweight fabric. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the volume inside the kite?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text{cm}^3$
hints:
The base of the kite is a square so all $4$ side faces are congruent rectangles. To find the area of each face, we can divide.

$\dfrac{7200}{4} = \blueD{1800} \text{ cm}^2$

$ \blueD{1800} \text{ cm}^2$ is the amount of fabric it takes to cover one face.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use the formula for the area of a rectangle to find the height of the kite. The width of the rectangle is $\purpleD{30}$.

$\begin{align}A & = lw \\\\
\blueD{1800} & = l\cdot \purpleD{30} \\\\
\greenD{60} & = l \end{align}$

Now we can use the length, width, and height to find the volume of the kite.

$\begin{align}V & = lwh \\\\
V & = \purpleD{30}\cdot \purpleD{30}\cdot \greenD{60} \\\\
V & =54{,}000\end{align}$
The volume of the kite is $54{,}000\text{ cm}^3$.

options.alt: A vertical rectangular prism with two of its top and bottom sides labeled as 30 cm each.

assessment_items.question_content:
Syrus is buying a tent with the dimensions shown below. The  volume inside the tent is $4.5\text{ m}^3$. Syrus isn't sure if the tent will be tall enough for him to stand inside.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the height of the tent?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints: We can use the volume  formula,  $V = \dfrac12bhl$, to find the height of the tent.

The volume is $\greenD{4.5}\text{ m}^3$. The base is $\purpleD{1.5}\text{ m}$ and the length is $\maroonD{3}\text{ m}$.

$\begin{align}\greenD V & =\dfrac12\purpleD bh\maroonD l \\\\
\greenD{4.5} & = \dfrac12 \cdot \purpleD{1.5}\cdot h \cdot \maroonD3 \\\\
4.5& = 2.25h \\\\
2& =h\end{align}$
The height of the tent is $2$ meters.

options.alt: A three-dimensional triangular prism with a right triangle on one of its sides. The base of the right triangle is labeled 1.5 meters, and the adjacent side is vertical, forming a right angle. The slanted side of the prism is labeled 3 meters. The prism extends backward, creating a solid shape with depth, shown by the dashed lines.

assessment_items.question_content:
Angela makes a pillow in the shape of a wedge to use for watching TV. The pillow is filled with $0.35\text{ m}^3$ of fluffy material.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the length of the pillow?**  
*Give an exact answer (do not round).*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints: We can use the volume  formula  $V = \dfrac12bhl$ to find the length of the pillow.

The volume is $\greenD{0.35}\text{ m}^3$. The base of the triangle is $\purpleD{1}\text{ m}$ and the height of the triangle is  $\maroonD{0.5}\text{ m}$.

$\begin{align}\greenD V & =\dfrac12\purpleD b\maroonD hl \\\\
\greenD{0.35} & = \dfrac12 \cdot \purpleD1\cdot \maroonD{0.5} \cdot l \\\\
0.35& = 0.25l \\\\
1.4& =l\end{align}$
The length of the pillow is $1.4$ meters.

options.alt: A three-dimensional triangular prism. The front face is a right triangle with one vertical side labeled 0.5 meters, one horizontal side labeled 1 meter, and the hypotenuse (the slanted side) is unlabeled, indicated with a question mark. The prism extends back from the triangle, giving it depth, with the back edges shown as dashed lines to represent the hidden parts. The right angle is marked in the corner where the vertical and horizontal sides meet. The prism has a slanted, elongated shape, with the triangular face forming the front.

assessment_items.question_content:
Emily convinced her mom to buy a giant box of her favorite cereal. Her mom doesn't think  the box will fit on their shelf. The volume of the box is $10{,}000$ cm$^3$. The base of the box is $25$ cm by $10$ cm.

**How tall is the box of cereal?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] cm
hints: We can use the volume  formula  $V = lwh$ to find the height of the box.

The volume of the box is $\greenD{10{,}000}$ cm$^3$. The base is $\purpleD{25}$ cm by $\maroonD{10}$ cm.

$\begin{align}\greenD V & =\purpleD l\maroonD w h\\\\
\greenD{10{,}000} & =\purpleD{25}\cdot \maroonD{10} \cdot h \\\\
10{,}000 & = 250h \\\\
40& =h\end{align}$
The height of the cereal box is $40$ cm.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cal's go cart has a gas tank with the dimensions shown below.  
He uses a gas can that holds $1$ liter of gas, to fill the go cart tank.  
*$1$ liter = $1000\text{ cm}^3$*

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many full gas cans will it take to fill the go cart's gas tank?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] cans
hints: The volume of the gas tank can be found with the formula $V = lwh$.

Let's find the volume of the gas tank.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}V & = lwh \\\\
V & = \blueD{40}\cdot \purpleD{25}\cdot \greenD{8} \\\\
V & =8000\end{align} $

To find the number of gas cans it will take to fill the tank we can divide. There are $1000\text{ cm}^3$ of gas in one can.

$\dfrac{8000}{1000} = 8$
It will take $8$ full cans of gas to fill the go cart's gas tank.

options.alt: A rectangular prism with length labeled as 40 cm, breadth labeled as 25 cm, and height labeled as 8 cm.

assessment_items.question_content:
Zach keeps his pet chameleon Pinky in a terrarium with the dimensions shown below.  There's sand in the bottom of the terrarium that reaches a height of $8$ centimeters.

[[☃ image 1]]

Zach gets a new terrarium for Pinky that is larger.  The base of the new terrarium is $25$ by $64$ centimeters. Zach moved the existing sand to the new terrarium.

**How deep will the sand be in the new terrarium?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] centimeters
hints: We need to find the volume of the sand in Pinky's terrarium. We can use the formula $V = lwh$.

The length of the terrarium is $\blueD{50}\text{ cm}$ and the width is $\purpleD{20}\text{ cm}$. The sand is $\greenD8\text{ cm}$ deep, so the height is $\greenD8$.



$\begin{align}V & = lwh \\\\
V & = \blueD{50}\cdot \purpleD{20}\cdot \greenD{8} \\\\
V & =\maroonD{8000}\end{align} $

To find out how deep the sand will be in the new terrarium, we can plug in the new length, $\goldD{64}\text{ cm}$, the new width, $\redD{25}\text{ cm}$ and the volume of the sand.

$\begin{align}V & = lwh \\\\
\maroonD{8000} & = \goldD{64} \cdot \redD{25} \cdot h \\\\
\maroonD{8000} & =1600h \\\\
5 & = h\end{align} $
The sand will be $5$ centimeters deep in the new terrarium.

options.alt: A rectangular prism with length of 50 cm and breadth of 20 cm.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two boxes have the same volume. One box has a base that is $5\text{ cm}$ by $5\text{ cm}$. The other box has a base that is $10\text{ cm}$ by $10\text{ cm}$. 

**How many times as tall is the box with the smaller base?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] times as tall
hints: We can use the formula $V = lwh$ to compare the volume in the two boxes.

First let's compare the volume of both boxes to see if they have the **same** height. To make it simple, let's use a height of $1$ centimeter.

First the box with the smaller base.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}V & = lwh \\\\
V & = 5\cdot 5\cdot 1 \\\\
V & =25\end{align} $

Now the box with the larger base

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}V & = lwh \\\\
V & = 10\cdot 10\cdot 1 \\\\
V & =100\end{align} $

We can set up an equation to find out how many times as tall the smaller box needs to be to have the same volume as the box with the larger base.

$\begin{align} 25\cdot h & = 100\\\\
h & = 4 \end{align}$
To have a volume of $100\text{ cm}^3$, the height of the box with the smaller base needs to be $4\text{ cm}$. The height of the box with the larger base is $1\text{ cm}$.
The box with the smaller base is $4$ times as tall.

assessment_items.question_content:
Aubrey has a new art box in the shape of a rectangular prism. The box is $12\text{ cm}$ by $20\text{ cm}$ by $5\text{ cm}$. The only thing in the art box is a new pink eraser with the dimensions shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the volume of the art box not taken up by the eraser?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}^3$
hints:
First let's find the volume of the art box.

$\begin{align}V & = lwh \\\\
V & =20\cdot12\cdot 5 \\\\
V & =1200\end{align} $

Now let's find the volume taken up by the eraser.

$\begin{align}V & = lwh \\\\
V & = \blueD{4}\cdot \purpleD{2}\cdot \greenD{0.5} \\\\
V & =4\end{align} $

We can subtract to find the volume of the box that is not taken up by the eraser.

$1200- 4 = 1196\text{ cm}^3$
The volume of the art box not taken up by the eraser is $1196\text{ cm}^3$.

options.alt: A three-dimensional rectangular prism. The box has a height of 0.5 cm, a width of 2 cm, and a length of 4 cm. The visible edges of the box are shown as solid lines, while the hidden edges are represented with dashed lines.

assessment_items.question_content:
A theater wants to build movable steps that they can use to go on and off the stage. They want the steps to have enough space inside so they can also be used to store props. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**How much space is inside the steps?**  
*Give an exact answer (do not round).*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}^3$
hints:
We can divide the end of the steps into $2$ rectangles. The width of each step is $0.2\text{ m}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangle on the left is $0.2\text{ m}$ by $0.3\text{ m}$. We can use the formula $A = lw$ to find the area.

$\begin{align}\blueD A & = lw \\\\
\blueD A & = 0.3\cdot 0.2 \\\\
\blueD A & = \blueD{0.06} \end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangle on the right is $0.2\text{ m}$ by $0.15\text{ m}$. We can use the formula $A = lw$ to find the area.

$\begin{align}\purpleD A & = lw \\\\
\purpleD A & = 0.2\cdot 0.15 \\\\
\purpleD A & = \purpleD{0.03} \end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

To find the total area for the end of the steps we can add the area of both rectangles together.

$\begin{align} A & = \blueD{0.06} + \purpleD{0.03} \\\\
A & = \greenD{0.09}  \end{align}$
To find the volume of the steps we can multiply the area of the end of the steps by the width of the steps

$\begin{align} V & = \text{area of the end} \cdot \maroonD h \\\\
V & = \greenD{0.09} \cdot \maroonD{0.5} \\\\
V& = 0.045\end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The volume of the steps is $0.045\text{ m}^3$.

options.alt: A 3 D shape formed by 2 blocks that share a common vertical side. The base formed has a combined length labeled as 0.4 m. The smaller block is of height 0.15 m and length 0.2 m. The height of the bigger block is 0.3 m. The width of the figure is labeled as 0.5 m.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joe is building a fort for his little brother out of boxes. He is wondering how much room there will be inside the fort.
 
[[☃ image 1]]

**How much space is inside the fort?**  
*Give an exact answer (do not round).*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}^3$
hints:
We can divide the end of the fort into $2$ rectangles. 

[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangle on the left is $0.5\text{ m}$ by $0.5\text{ m}$. We can use the formula $A = lw$ to find the area.

$\begin{align}\blueD A & = lw \\\\
\blueD A & = 0.5\cdot 0.5\\\\
\blueD A & = \blueD{0.25} \end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangle on the right is $1\text{ m}$ by $0.75\text{ m}$. We can use the formula $A = lw$ to find the area.

$\begin{align}\purpleD A & = lw \\\\
\purpleD A & = 1\cdot 0.75\\\\
\purpleD A & = \purpleD{0.75} \end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

To find the total area for the end of the fort we can add the area of both rectangles together.

$\begin{align} A & = \blueD{0.25} + \purpleD{0.75} \\\\
A & = \greenD{1}  \end{align}$
To find the volume of the fort we can multiply the area of the end of the fort by the width of the fort.

$\begin{align} V & = \text{area of the end} \cdot \maroonD w \\\\
V & = \greenD{1} \cdot \maroonD{0.5} \\\\
V& = 0.5 \end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The volume inside the fort is $0.5\text{ m}^3$.

options.alt: A three-dimensional block with a stepped shape. It consists of two rectangular sections, one smaller and one larger, stacked together. The smaller section, on the left, has a height and width of 0.5 meters. The larger section, to the right, has a height of 0.75 meters and a width of 1 meter. The total length of the shape from left to right is 1.5 meters. The front and top surfaces are visible, with dashed lines.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lacey's mom makes her a birthday cake in the shape of an "L". Lacey loves frosting, so her mom covers the entire outside of the cake in frosting, even the bottom of the cake.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How much space does Lacey's mom cover in frosting?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}^2$
hints: The surface area of the the cake is made up of rectangles. The formula for finding the area of a rectangle is $A = lw$.

We can divide the top of the cake into $2$ rectangles. 

[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangle on the left is $30\text{ cm}$ by $10\text{ cm}$.

$\begin{align}\blueD A & = lw \\\\
\blueD A & = 30\cdot 10\\\\
\blueD A & = \blueD{300} \end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

There is a rectangle this size on the top and bottom of the cake, so we can multiply to find the area of both.

$\blueD{300} \cdot 2 = 600$

The rectangle on the right is $12\text{ cm}$ by $10\text{ cm}$. We can use the formula $A = lw$ to find the area.

$\begin{align}\purpleD A & = lw \\\\
\purpleD A & = 12\cdot 10 \\\\
\purpleD A & = \purpleD{120} \end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

There is a rectangle this size on the top and bottom of the cake, so we can multiply to find the area of both.

$\purpleD{120} \cdot 2 = 240$

Now we need to add in the area of the rectangles that make the sides of the cake. Let's look at the sides of the large rectangle first.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldD{30\cdot 5 = 150}$  
$\greenD{10 \cdot 5 =50}$  
$\maroonD{20 \cdot 5= 100}$

The total for these $3$ surfaces is $\tealD{300}$.

Now let's look at the $3$ remaining rectangles.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldD{12 \cdot 5 = 60}$  
$\greenD{10 \cdot 5 =50}$  
$\maroonD{22 \cdot 5= 110}$

The total for these $3$ surfaces is $\redD{220}$.
The surface area of Lacey's cake is $1360\text{ cm}^2$.

options.alt: A three-dimensional L-shaped object with labeled dimensions. The vertical section of the L is 30 cm tall and 10 cm wide. The horizontal section extends outward from the base of the vertical part, with a length of 22 cm and a height of 10 cm. The width of the horizontal section is 5 cm. Additional labels indicate that the inner vertical segment of the L is 20 cm high.
